conventional_view: Most CA public-agency/Prop 218 counsel would conclude the Surcharge is legally vulnerable and likely violates article XIII D, § 6(b)(1)-(3) as construed in Capistrano Taxpayers Assn. v. City of San Juan Capistrano (2015). The district's own admissions — that the $0.85 figure is derived partly from "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than cost allocation, and that rebate proceeds mostly flow to non-payers — track almost exactly the defects Capistrano found fatal, and the conservation mandate of article X, § 2 / Water Code § 375 is generally understood not to override Prop 218's proportionality requirement (a later, specific constitutional provision) or to convert an incentive markup into a cost-based charge. Water delivery charges are also settled as "property-related fees" under Bighorn-Desert View Water Agency v. Verjil, so the district's alternative "not a property-related fee" argument is considered weak. The conventional advice is that the portion of the surcharge cost-justified by the 1.6x marginal-cost finding is likely defensible, but the excess (incentive-priced) increment and the cross-subsidy to the rebate program create refund exposure and should be restructured — e.g., funding rebates/education from a non-fee source or general fund/voter-approved measure, and re-deriving tier rates strictly from marginal cost of service.
disfavored_position: The Water Resilience Surcharge is a valid article XIII D property-related fee that satisfies § 6(b): the tiered rate is anchored to the 1.6x marginal-cost-of-service finding in the rate study, Capistrano's rejection of that particular tiered structure does not categorically bar conservation-based inclining-block rates so long as a cost nexus exists, the rebate, recycled-water, and education expenditures are properly attributable to the cost of maintaining system-wide drought reliability and peaking capacity (and thus benefit high-tier payers even if disbursed to other customers), and article X, § 2's conservation mandate should be read together with, rather than subordinated to, article XIII D so as to validate conservation-incentive pricing. In the further alternative, the surcharge is not subject to § 6(b) at all — either because it is not a "fee or charge" within the meaning of article XIII D, or because it qualifies as a valid regulatory/conservation charge outside Proposition 26's tax definition — such that no voter approval was required and no refund liability arises.
